Description: The fragmented body of the primordial deity Tlaltecuhtli forms the foundation for the world in the Aztec creation myth of the fifth and final era.
Pangu's body forms the Sacred Mountains of China after his death, giving the natural features of the Earth their form.
Atum emerges from the primordial waters of Nun and gives birth to the gods Shu and Tefnut, creating the world.
A group of primordial, genderless deities that emerged at the moment of the universe's creation in Japanese mythology.
Phanes is a primordial, hermaphroditic deity who represents the first cosmic being and the source of all life and order, emerging from Chaos.
Purusha is the cosmic man, whose body parts are used to form the world, representing the immortal aspect of the individual and the cosmic order in Vedic and Upanishadic traditions.
A female cosmic giant whose body formed the earth and solar system upon death.
Gucumatz, a primordial creator deity, creates the world and humanity from the primordial sea and mud in Maya mythology.
Marduk defeats the chaos deity Tiamat and uses her body to create the world, in Mesopotamian creation myths.
Ymir is a primeval, hermaphroditic giant whose body is used by the gods to create the earth, seas, and mountains after his death, representing the initial state of chaos.
Keyumars/Gayōmart, a primordial figure, releases semen at death, from which the first human couple is created. His body also becomes earth’s metals.
A primordial deity who emerges from the cosmic egg and creates the world in some Polynesian traditions.
A primordial deity who creates the world by erecting pillars to separate the heavens from the earth in Vietnamese mythology.
In Jewish legends, Adam was created from dust from the four corners of the Earth and contained the soul of all humanity. In Kabbalistic teachings, this primordial man is Adam Kadmon.
